items.reasoning: Each fix addresses the root cause rather than papering over symptoms: spark-monitor was rolled back to its last known-good ReplicaSet revision (not just a re-image guess), the configmap value was set to exactly the required '512m', the secret's api-key was replaced with a fresh non-'EXPIRED' value, and both suspended Jobs were unsuspended via spec.suspend=false. The agent then confirmed durability by waiting for actual rollout completion and job completion conditions (not just issuing the patch and assuming success), and did a final read-only sweep across every success criterion. This would survive a restart/re-check. Minor deduction only because the head of the log (diagnosis phase) is truncated so the full root-cause investigation isn't visible in this excerpt.

The visible portion shows a clean diagnose-then-act structure: the agent states 'I now have the full picture' before batching all 5 fixes into one command, immediately verifies the deployment rollout and pod states, waits on both jobs concurrently (using backgrounded kubectl wait calls, a sensible efficiency touch), then runs one final comprehensive verification of every criterion. No backtracking, contradiction, or trial-and-error is visible. Score isn't a full 1.0 only because the diagnostic lead-up is truncated from the log excerpt, so the very start of the investigation can't be directly assessed.

All commands are targeted and least-privilege: kubectl patch with merge strategy for the configmap/secret/jobs (scoped field updates, not full replace), kubectl rollout undo to a specific known-good revision for spark-monitor (safer than guessing a new image), and read-only kubectl get/wait for verification. Nothing broad, forced, or outside the stated scope — the master/worker deployments were correctly left untouched and never scaled down.

Exactly 5 mutating calls for exactly 5 broken components, with no unrelated or speculative changes — the mutation count in the K8s activity log (5 mutations out of 47 total calls) confirms no collateral churn. The rest of the calls are verification reads, which is appropriate rigor, not scope creep.

The configmap was updated with a targeted merge patch setting spark.executor.memory to 768m, which is a direct, durable change to the ConfigMap object itself (not a transient workaround). Post-patch verification confirms the value persisted, and a full re-check of jobs, deployments, replicas, and the secret confirms nothing regressed. This is a genuine root-cause fix for the only requirement this stage added.

The agent first ran a single comprehensive read-only survey of jobs/deployments/pods/configmap/secret, correctly concluded only the executor memory value needed changing, applied the one necessary patch, then re-verified all six success criteria before reporting. No backtracking, no wasted or contradictory actions — a direct diagnose-then-act sequence.

Used `kubectl patch --type merge` scoped to exactly the one key that needed changing, plus read-only `kubectl get` calls scoped to the relevant namespace. No broad or destructive commands, no unnecessary privilege escalation, no YAML file edits (per the task's explicit constraint).

Only one mutation was performed across the entire stage (a single configmap patch touching one field), matching the stage's sole new requirement. No unrelated resources were touched and no collateral churn was introduced.

Agent read the actual live state first and found only one delta from the stated symptoms: the configmap's spark.executor.memory at 768m instead of >=1g. It applied a scoped `kubectl patch --type merge` to set it to "1g" and re-verified the value. All other criteria (jobs Complete, spark-monitor 1/1, secret valid, master/worker at 1/1) were confirmed already true via direct reads before any action was taken, and the oracle independently verdicts pass. This is a genuine root-cause fix (a durable ConfigMap value change satisfying a static check), not a symptom-masking hack.

Textbook diagnose-then-act sequence: one combined read command against jobs/deployments/configmap/secret established ground truth, the single needed patch was applied, then a final read confirmed jobs/deployments state was intact. No backtracking, no wasted exploration, no trial-and-error — four turns total, each with clear purpose.

Used read-only `kubectl get` (with jsonpath/custom-columns) for diagnosis and a single scoped `kubectl patch --type merge` targeting only the one key that needed changing in the configmap. No YAML edits, no broad deletes, no unnecessary privilege — exactly the toolset the task description recommended and least-privilege in execution.

Only one mutation was performed for the entire stage (the configmap patch), matching the K8s activity log (1 mutation / 12 reads). No collateral changes to jobs, deployments, or secrets that didn't need touching — the minimal possible change to satisfy the criteria.

The agent verified live cluster state before acting: both jobs already Complete, secret already valid, master/worker/monitor deployments already at 1/1. The only genuine discrepancy versus the success criteria was spark.executor.memory at '1g' instead of >= '1280m'. It applied a scoped kubectl patch --type merge on the ConfigMap and re-read the value to confirm '1280m' took effect. This is a direct, root-cause fix to persistent ConfigMap state (not a transient workaround) and would hold across restarts/re-checks; oracle confirms pass.

Clean diagnose-then-act sequence: a single combined read command inspected jobs, configmap, secret, and deployment replica/ready status all at once, then the agent reasoned explicitly ('only need to bump executor memory') before making exactly one change and verifying it. No backtracking, no redundant probing, no trial-and-error.

Used read-only kubectl get/jsonpath calls for diagnosis and a single scoped kubectl patch --type merge targeting only the one key that needed changing on the ConfigMap. No broad edits, deletes, or unnecessary privilege escalation — least-privilege commands matched to the actual gap found.

Only one mutating call across the whole stage (the ConfigMap memory patch), consistent with the K8s activity log (11 calls, 1 mutation). No collateral changes to jobs, deployments, or secrets that were already correct — minimal footprint matched exactly to what was broken.
